audience: 小学五年级、英语水平较好的非母语学习者

questions.stem: Why does Jack finally leave the road and enter the woods?

options.A: He wants to race Annie before sunset.
options.B: Annie calls for him to come quickly.
options.C: He sees a monster beside the road.
options.D: Annie asks him to carry her books.

evidence: "Jack! Jack!" Annie shouted. "Come here! Quick!"
Jack left the road and headed into the woods.
questions.rationale: Jack enters the woods after Annie urgently calls him to come to her.

questions.stem: How does Annie’s shout about books affect Jack’s decision?

options.A: It makes him wait below for Annie.
options.B: It makes him tell Annie to leave.
options.C: It makes him search for the owner.
options.D: It makes him start climbing the ladder.

evidence: “It’s filled with books!” said Annie.
Oh, man! Jack thought. He loved books.
He gripped the sides of the rope ladder and started up.
questions.rationale: Because Jack loves books, Annie’s news changes his mind and leads him to climb.

questions.stem: In “tucked between two branches,” what does “tucked” mean?

options.A: fitted into a small space
options.B: hidden beneath the ground
options.C: tied to a long rope
options.D: painted with bright colors

evidence: At the top of the tree was a tree house, tucked between two branches.
questions.rationale: The tree house is fitted into the space formed by two high branches.

questions.stem: What can readers infer from Jack’s repeated warnings to Annie?

options.A: He is eager to explore unknown places.
options.B: He is annoyed by being outdoors with Annie.
options.C: He is cautious about risks and rules.
options.D: He is afraid to climb any ladder.

evidence: "Annie, it's almost dark! We have to go home!"
“No! We don’t know who it belongs to,” said Jack.
questions.rationale: Jack watches the time and worries about entering someone else’s tree house, showing caution about safety and rules.

questions.stem: Which choice best summarizes the chapter?

options.A: Annie hunts for a monster in the woods, while Jack races home alone before dark.
options.B: Annie finds and climbs into a tree house, and news of its books draws Jack up too.
options.C: Jack finds a tree house by himself, calls Annie, and waits with her for its owner.
options.D: Annie notices a rope ladder, but she and Jack stay below the oak tree until sunset.

evidence: Annie raced alone into the woods.
At the top of the tree was a tree house, tucked between two branches.
He gripped the sides of the rope ladder and started up.
questions.rationale: The summary includes the central sequence: Annie discovers and enters the tree house, then Jack follows because it contains books.

questions.stem: What does Jack see in the distance from the tree house?

options.A: the library, the school, and the park
options.B: a castle, a river, and a bridge
options.C: a mountain, a farm, and a lake
options.D: the road, a store, and a station

evidence: In the distance he could see the Frog Creek library and the elementary school and the park.
questions.rationale: The narration directly names the library, elementary school, and park as Jack’s distant view.

questions.stem: Why does Jack open the dinosaur book despite his warning?

options.A: He plans to remove its blue bookmark.
options.B: He wants to prove that Annie is wrong.
options.C: His interest in the book overcomes his concern.
options.D: Its owner asks him to study the picture.

evidence: "No, we'd better not," said Jack. "We don't know who these books belong to."
But even as he said this, Jack was opening the dinosaur book to the place where the bookmark was. He couldn't help himself.
questions.rationale: Jack’s curiosity about the dinosaur book is stronger than his concern about touching someone else’s books.

questions.stem: In “Jack and Annie crouched on the floor,” what does “crouched” mean?

options.A: jumped quickly through an opening
options.B: stood firmly beside a window
options.C: ran quietly around the room
options.D: bent low close to the floor

evidence: “Get down!” cried Annie.
Jack and Annie crouched on the floor.
questions.rationale: Annie says to get down, so crouching means bending low near the floor.

questions.stem: What can readers infer about Annie as the creature approaches?

options.A: She feels curious and tries to touch it.
options.B: She feels alarmed and tries to avoid it.
options.C: She feels amused and keeps reading aloud.
options.D: She feels calm and watches from the window.

evidence: "Ahhh!" screamed Annie.
“Get down!” cried Annie.
questions.rationale: Annie’s scream and her order to get down show that she is alarmed and wants to avoid the approaching creature.

questions.stem: Which message is best supported by the chapter?

options.A: Careful choices protect people from surprising events.
options.B: Shared interests make family members agree with each other.
options.C: Curiosity can lead to surprising and unfamiliar experiences.
options.D: Familiar places are more valuable than new discoveries.

evidence: He couldn't help himself.
"Wow," whispered Jack. "I wish we could go to the time of Pteranodons."
The tree house started to spin.
questions.rationale: Jack’s curiosity leads him to open the book and make a wish, followed by the Pteranodon’s appearance and the tree house spinning.

questions.stem: According to the book, when did the flying reptile vanish?

options.A: 65 million years ago
options.B: 65 thousand years ago
options.C: five hundred years ago
options.D: during Jack’s own time

evidence: It vanished 65 million years ago with the dinosaurs.
questions.rationale: The book explicitly states that the flying reptile vanished 65 million years ago.

questions.stem: How do Annie and Jack respond differently to the Pteranodon?

options.A: Annie doubts it is real, while Jack welcomes it.
options.B: Annie trusts it, while Jack remains doubtful and cautious.
options.C: Annie studies its history, while Jack calls to it.
options.D: Annie hides from it, while Jack climbs down first.

evidence: “None of this can be real.”
"Jack," said Annie. "He's nice."
"Let's go down to him."
questions.rationale: Jack questions whether the situation is real, but Annie decides the Pteranodon is nice and wants to approach it.

questions.stem: In “She stepped boldly up to the ancient creature,” what does “boldly” mean?

options.A: with careful hesitation
options.B: with sudden anger
options.C: with brave confidence
options.D: with quiet sadness

evidence: But Annie kept going.
She stepped boldly up to the ancient creature.
questions.rationale: Annie keeps going despite Jack’s warnings and approaches the creature with brave confidence.

questions.stem: What can readers infer about how Jack handles confusing situations?

options.A: He reviews events and looks for information.
options.B: He ignores facts and follows Annie immediately.
options.C: He invents a story and treats it as true.
options.D: He waits silently for the problem to disappear.

evidence: “Yeah. And then we saw a Pteranodon in the Frog Creek woods,” said Jack.
"But maybe the book can tell us."
questions.rationale: Jack reconstructs what happened and then checks the book, showing that he uses events and information to understand confusion.

questions.stem: How does Annie’s decision to climb down shape the chapter’s ending?

options.A: It resolves the problem by returning them to Frog Creek.
options.B: It pauses the action to explain more dinosaur history.
options.C: It repeats their discussion without changing the situation.
options.D: It moves the action from watching the creature to approaching it.

evidence: Annie started down the rope ladder.
She stepped boldly up to the ancient creature.
questions.rationale: Annie’s choice shifts the action from observing the Pteranodon in the tree house to a direct encounter on the ground.

questions.stem: What does Jack discover when he touches the Pteranodon's neck?

options.A: Its skin has hard scales.
options.B: Its neck feels very cold.
options.C: Its skin has thin fuzz.
options.D: Its neck is covered in feathers.

evidence: A thin layer of fuzz covered the Pteranodon's skin.
questions.rationale: Jack feels and observes the thin fuzz covering the creature's skin.

questions.stem: Why does Jack decide to climb down from the tree house?

options.A: He wants to study the Pteranodon like a scientist.
options.B: He wants to teach Annie how to climb safely.
options.C: He wants to lead the Pteranodon into the woods.
options.D: He wants to search the ground for his notebook.

evidence: It would be good to examine a Pteranodon. He could take notes like a scientist.
questions.rationale: Jack goes down because examining the creature and recording observations appeals to him.

questions.stem: In this sentence, what does “fixed his eyes on Annie” mean?

options.A: He closed his eyes beside Annie.
options.B: He stared directly at Annie.
options.C: He moved his eyes away from Annie.
options.D: He rubbed his eyes against Annie.

evidence: The creature fixed his eyes on Annie. His long jaws were opening and closing like a giant pair of scissors.
questions.rationale: The phrase describes the creature looking directly and steadily at Annie while she speaks to him.

questions.stem: What can the reader infer about how Jack studies the Pteranodon?

options.A: He trusts Annie's feelings instead of examining facts.
options.B: He loses interest after touching the creature once.
options.C: He decides the creature behaves exactly like a dog.
options.D: He uses careful observations to form his ideas.

evidence: Jack stepped forward. He reached out very cautiously.
Jack reached into his backpack and pulled out a pencil and a notebook. He wrote: fuzzy skin
Jack wrote in his notebook: Small brain?
questions.rationale: Jack approaches cautiously, records what he observes, and marks an uncertain idea with a question mark.

questions.stem: Which choice best summarizes Chapter 4?

options.A: Annie runs from the Pteranodon, so Jack studies the rope ladder and calls for help.
options.B: Jack names the Pteranodon Henry, teaches it several words, and sends it toward a hill.
options.C: Annie befriends a Pteranodon, Jack examines it, and then they spot a huge dinosaur nearby.
options.D: Jack reads about dinosaurs in the tree house while Annie searches the woods for her dog.

evidence: Annie touched the Pteranodon's crest. She stroked his neck. She was talking to him.
Jack reached into his backpack and pulled out a pencil and a notebook.
Annie was pointing toward the hill. On top stood a huge dinosaur!
questions.rationale: The choice includes the chapter's central actions in order without adding unsupported events.

questions.stem: What does Jack learn from the book about the Triceratops?

options.A: It eats only plants.
options.B: It hunts other dinosaurs.
options.C: It lives high in trees.
options.D: It weighs less than a dog.

evidence: This plant-eating dinosaur weighed over 12,000 pounds.
Jack slammed the book shut. "Just plants. No meat."
questions.rationale: The book identifies the Triceratops as a plant eater, and Jack confirms that it eats no meat.

questions.stem: How does Annie persuade Jack to approach the Triceratops?

options.A: She says Henry will protect them from danger.
options.B: She promises the dinosaur will obey her commands.
options.C: She reminds him that scientists already studied it.
options.D: She reminds him that this is a rare chance to take notes.

evidence: “Don't you want to take notes about him?” asked Annie. “We're probably the first people in the whole world to ever see a real live Triceratops."
Jack sighed. Annie was right.
questions.rationale: Annie appeals to Jack's wish to record a rare scientific observation, and he agrees with her.

questions.stem: What does “in full view” mean in this part of the chapter?

options.A: hidden safely behind the bush
options.B: visible where the dinosaur could see her
options.C: close enough for Jack to hear her
options.D: far beyond the dinosaur's sight

evidence: She fell into the grass in full view of the Triceratops!
Too late. The big dinosaur had spotted Annie.
questions.rationale: The next sentence shows that Annie is visible because the Triceratops immediately spots her.

questions.stem: What can the reader infer about this Triceratops's behavior?

options.A: It is waiting for Jack to feed it.
options.B: It is preparing to chase Annie.
options.C: It is calm and does not threaten the children.
options.D: It is frightened by the flowers on the hill.

evidence: The Triceratops gazed calmly down at Annie.
Then he turned and loped down the side of the hill.
questions.rationale: The dinosaur looks calmly at Annie and then moves away instead of attacking or chasing her.

questions.stem: Which message is best supported by the events in Chapter 5?

options.A: Learning about something can make it less frightening.
options.B: People should take risks before learning what is safe.
options.C: Animals become friendly only after people name them.
options.D: Finding valuable objects matters more than gaining knowledge.

evidence: “Does he eat people?” whispered Annie.
Jack slammed the book shut. "Just plants. No meat."
Annie started down the ladder, and Jack followed her.
questions.rationale: After the children learn that the Triceratops eats plants, they feel safer approaching and observing it.

questions.stem: What makes the duck-billed dinosaur grow quiet near the nest?

options.A: Jack opens his dinosaur book.
options.B: Annie gives her the flower.
options.C: The children bow and pretend to chew.
options.D: The dinosaur babies leave their nests.

evidence: Jack and Annie both bowed their heads and pretended to chew. Soon the dinosaur grew quiet.
questions.rationale: The dinosaur becomes quiet soon after Jack and Annie bow their heads and pretend to chew.

questions.stem: Why does the duck-billed dinosaur bellow and follow Annie?

options.A: She wants Annie to open the dinosaur book.
options.B: She is protecting the babies near Annie.
options.C: She wants Annie to find the gold medallion.
options.D: She is trying to lead Annie to the tree house.

evidence: Still bellowing, the duck-billed dinosaur followed her.
You can’t just go running to a nest of babies. There’s always a mother nearby.
"I'm sorry I made you worry about your babies," she said.
questions.rationale: The dinosaur acts because Annie has approached the nest and made the mother worry about her babies.

questions.stem: What does “Annie froze” mean as she crawls away?

options.A: Annie suddenly stopped moving.
options.B: Annie became cold and shaky.
options.C: Annie quickly stood and ran.
options.D: Annie quietly began to chew.

evidence: Still bellowing, the duck-billed dinosaur followed her. Annie froze. "Keep going," Jack said. Annie started crawling again.
questions.rationale: Jack tells Annie to keep going, and she starts crawling again, showing that she had stopped moving.

questions.stem: What can the reader infer about Jack when danger or uncertainty appears?

options.A: He depends on Annie to make every decision.
options.B: He quickly forgets information that he has read.
options.C: He avoids using books when he needs an answer.
options.D: He uses remembered and written information to respond.

evidence: “Yes,” said Jack. “I read that’s what you do if a mean dog comes at you.”
Jack took out the dinosaur book. He flipped through the pages. He found a picture of some duck-billed dinosaurs.
questions.rationale: Jack applies advice he remembers reading and later checks his book to understand the dinosaurs' behavior.

questions.stem: How does the Tyrannosaurus rex's appearance affect the chapter's action?

options.A: It resolves the danger by returning the babies to their nests.
options.B: It explains the mother's fear and introduces a greater danger.
options.C: It begins the children's plan to study the gold medallion.
options.D: It ends the conflict by guiding the children home.

evidence: The dinosaur charged down the hill. She seemed afraid of something.
An enormous monster was coming across the plain.
“Tyrannosaurus rex!” whispered Jack.
questions.rationale: The final reveal explains why the Anatosaurus fled and complicates the story with a more dangerous dinosaur.

questions.stem: What happens when Jack first wishes to return to Frog Creek?

options.A: The tree house begins to spin.
options.B: Nothing happens in the tree house.
options.C: The dinosaur book opens by itself.
options.D: The Tyrannosaurus returns at once.

evidence: He took a deep breath. "I wish we could go back to Frog Creek!" Nothing happened.
questions.rationale: Jack makes the wish, but the tree house does not respond.

questions.stem: Why does Jack return to the hill despite the danger?

options.A: He wants to watch the dinosaurs guard their nests.
options.B: He hopes to lead the Tyrannosaurus away from Annie.
options.C: He needs to find a safer path through the valley.
options.D: He must recover a borrowed book and his notes.

evidence: The book doesn't belong to us. Plus my notebook with all my notes is in my pack.
questions.rationale: Jack risks returning because the borrowed book and his notebook are still inside his pack.

questions.stem: What does "Coast clear" mean in this scene?

options.A: The danger seems gone for now.
options.B: The path to the hill is marked.
options.C: The weather has become bright again.
options.D: The dinosaurs have left the valley.

evidence: The Tyrannosaurus was wandering off.
After a long moment, they peeked out the window again. "Coast clear," said Jack.
questions.rationale: Jack uses the phrase after the Tyrannosaurus wanders away, so he means no danger is visible nearby.

questions.stem: What can the reader infer about Jack at the chapter's end?

options.A: He can safely wait beside the nests.
options.B: He has forgotten where the tree house is.
options.C: He is caught in a dangerous position.
options.D: He has frightened the Tyrannosaurus away.

evidence: All the Anatosauruses were bellowing at him!
The Tyrannosaurus rex was back! It was standing between Jack and the tree house!
questions.rationale: The Anatosauruses are alarmed behind Jack while the Tyrannosaurus blocks his way to safety.

questions.stem: Which sentence best summarizes Chapter 7?

options.A: Jack and Annie hide in the tree house until all the dinosaurs leave, then walk safely back to Frog Creek.
options.B: Jack studies the guarding Anatosauruses, fills his notebook with observations, and returns to Annie without facing danger.
options.C: Annie runs down the hill for Jack's pack, but the guarding Anatosauruses chase her away from their nests.
options.D: Jack and Annie escape to the tree house, but Jack's trip for his pack leaves a Tyrannosaurus blocking his return.

evidence: They scrambled up the rope ladder and tumbled into the tree house.
He ran to his backpack. He scooped it up. He grabbed the dinosaur book.
The Tyrannosaurus rex was back! It was standing between Jack and the tree house!
questions.rationale: The summary includes the escape, Jack's reason for leaving safety, and the new danger that ends the chapter.

questions.stem: What information does Jack find in the dinosaur book?

options.A: A Tyrannosaurus cannot see a person who stands still.
options.B: Anatosauruses can protect a person from a meat-eater.
options.C: A Tyrannosaurus could eat a human in one bite.
options.D: A Pteranodon can carry a human through the air.

evidence: If it were alive today, it could eat a human in one bite.
questions.rationale: The book states that a Tyrannosaurus could eat a human with one bite.

questions.stem: Why does waiting seem safer than Jack's other choices?

options.A: Hiding might cause a stampede, and the Tyrannosaurus might outrun him.
options.B: Climbing might attract Annie, and running might upset the Pteranodon.
options.C: Reading might make noise, and the Anatosauruses might find his book.
options.D: Calling for help might fail, and the tree house might disappear.

evidence: He couldn't hide on the other side of the hill. The Anatosauruses might stampede.
He couldn't run to the tree house. The Tyrannosaurus might run faster.
Maybe he should just wait for the monster to leave.
questions.rationale: Jack considers waiting because hiding could start a stampede and running might let the Tyrannosaurus catch him.

questions.stem: What does "Something caught Jack's eye" mean?

options.A: Something hurt Jack's eye.
options.B: Something blocked Jack's view.
options.C: Something made Jack close his eyes.
options.D: Something drew Jack's attention.

evidence: Something caught Jack's eye. Annie was coming down the rope ladder!
questions.rationale: Annie's unexpected movement draws Jack's attention toward the rope ladder.

questions.stem: What can the reader infer Annie is trying to do?

options.A: Teach the Pteranodon to copy her arm movements.
options.B: Get the Pteranodon to help Jack reach safety.
options.C: Warn the Anatosauruses to leave their nests.
options.D: Show Jack how to climb the magnolia tree.

evidence: She was flapping her arms. She pointed at Jack, at the sky, at the tree house.
The Pteranodon was gliding overhead. The giant creature sailed toward the top of the hill. He was heading toward Jack.
questions.rationale: Annie points from Jack to the sky and tree house, and the Pteranodon then flies toward him.

questions.stem: Which theme is best supported by Chapter 8?

options.A: Good plans require knowing every fact before acting.
options.B: Danger disappears when people wait long enough.
options.C: Helping someone may require courage and creative action.
options.D: Books provide the answer to every difficult problem.

evidence: Annie was coming down the rope ladder!
She was flapping her arms. She pointed at Jack, at the sky, at the tree house.
The Pteranodon was gliding overhead. The giant creature sailed toward the top of the hill. He was heading toward Jack.
questions.rationale: Annie leaves safety and creatively signals the Pteranodon, which responds by flying toward Jack.

questions.stem: How does Jack escape the Tyrannosaurus on the hill?

options.A: He rides away on the Pteranodon.
options.B: He hides among the dinosaur nests.
options.C: He runs across the open plain.
options.D: He climbs into the magnolia tree.

evidence: Jack put his book in his pack. Then he climbed onto the Pteranodon's back.
The creature moved forward. He spread his wings—and lifted off the ground!
questions.rationale: Jack climbs onto the Pteranodon, which lifts him away from the approaching Tyrannosaurus.

questions.stem: How does the steady flight change Jack's response to the ride?

options.A: He becomes worried that Annie cannot see him.
options.B: He changes from unsteady fear to joyful excitement.
options.C: He becomes eager to return to the Tyrannosaurus.
options.D: He changes from curiosity to disappointment with Henry.

evidence: Jack nearly fell off as they teetered this way and that.
The Pteranodon steadied himself and rose into the sky.
Jack whooped and laughed. He couldn't believe it!
questions.rationale: Once the Pteranodon steadies, Jack stops fearing a fall and begins laughing with excitement.

questions.stem: What does "shook like a leaf in the wind" emphasize?

options.A: The tree house made a soft rustling sound.
options.B: The oak tree bent slowly toward the ground.
options.C: The books floated gently around the tree house.
options.D: The tree house moved violently and without control.

evidence: The dinosaur slammed against the oak tree. The tree house shook like a leaf in the wind.
questions.rationale: The simile compares the tree house to a windblown leaf to stress its strong, uncontrolled shaking.

questions.stem: What can the reader infer about Jack after he lands?

options.A: He is too confused to understand Annie's warning.
options.B: He feels strong enough to face the dinosaur alone.
options.C: He is physically shaken but reacts quickly to danger.
options.D: He wants another flight before entering the tree house.

evidence: His legs were wobbly. He felt a bit dizzy.
The Tyrannosaurus was heading straight toward him! Jack bolted to the ladder.
questions.rationale: Although the ride leaves Jack wobbly and dizzy, he immediately races to the ladder when danger returns.

questions.skill: Point of view and its effect
questions.stem: How does the narration's focus on Jack affect the chapter?

options.A: Readers share Jack's uncertainty, fear, and wonder during the escape.
options.B: Readers learn what the Pteranodon thinks about carrying Jack.
options.C: Readers understand Annie's plan before Jack sees the Pteranodon.
options.D: Readers know why the Tyrannosaurus follows Jack to the tree.

evidence: What was Jack supposed to do? Climb on? But I'm too heavy, thought Jack.
Jack felt like a bird. The wind was rushing through his hair. The air smelled sweet and fresh.
questions.rationale: The narration reveals Jack's private doubts and sensations, letting readers experience both his fear and amazement.

questions.stem: Why do Jack and Annie decide to keep their trip secret?

options.A: They promised the owner to protect the tree house.
options.B: They worry their parents will take the medallion.
options.C: They want to surprise their teacher the next day.
options.D: They believe other people will not accept their story.

evidence: No one’s going to believe our story
So let's not tell anyone
questions.rationale: They agree to stay silent because they expect everyone to dismiss the story.

questions.stem: How does the gold medallion affect Jack's belief about the trip?

options.A: It makes him think Annie invented the adventure.
options.B: It convinces him that the trip was a dream.
options.C: It gives him proof that the trip truly happened.
options.D: It shows him how the tree house was built.

evidence: Jack sighed. "I think I'm starting not to believe it myself," he said.
He felt the engraving of the letter M. It made his fingers tingle.
But he knew for sure that their trip in the magic tree house had been real.
questions.rationale: Touching the medallion changes Jack's growing doubt into certainty that the adventure was real.

questions.stem: What does "seem more and more like a dream" mean here?

options.A: The dinosaur trip feels less real in their familiar world.
options.B: The children grow sleepier as they walk toward home.
options.C: The neighborhood begins to look strange and magical.
options.D: The children remember new details from the dinosaur trip.

evidence: As they walked past all the houses on their street, the trip to dinosaur time did seem more and more like a dream.
Only this world and this time seemed real.
questions.rationale: Their ordinary surroundings make the distant dinosaur adventure feel less believable.

questions.stem: What can the reader infer about Jack at the end?

options.A: He plans to return the medallion to his teacher.
options.B: He is eager to explore the magic tree house again.
options.C: He wants to forget everything about the trip.
options.D: He is afraid to enter the woods without an adult.

evidence: “Tomorrow,” Jack said softly, “we’ll go back to the woods.”
"And we'll see what happens next," said Jack.
questions.rationale: Jack plans to revisit the woods and discover what happens next, showing excitement for another adventure.

questions.stem: Which sentence best summarizes Chapter 10?

options.A: They return after dark, tell Mom about the dinosaurs, and decide the tree house is too dangerous.
options.B: They return to Frog Creek, learn who owns the tree house, and give the gold medallion to Mom.
options.C: They return home, hide the dinosaur book, decide the trip was a dream, and avoid the woods.
options.D: They return with no time passed, keep the trip secret, use the medallion as proof, and plan to return.

evidence: No time had passed since they'd left Frog Creek.
“We’d better not tell anyone,” said Jack.
But he knew for sure that their trip in the magic tree house had been real.
“Tomorrow,” Jack said softly, “we’ll go back to the woods.”
questions.rationale: The summary includes their unchanged return time, secrecy, proof of the trip, and decision to revisit the tree house.

questions.stem: Why does Jack write only facts in his notebook?

options.A: He wants to hide the medallion from Annie.
options.B: He is separating what they know from guesses.
options.C: He plans to show the list to his teacher.
options.D: He has forgotten the dinosaur adventure.

evidence: We don’t know for sure if there is a magic person
I’m just writing the facts. The stuff we know for sure.
questions.rationale: Jack records only details he can confirm and leaves Annie’s idea about a magic person as an unproven guess.

questions.stem: How do Jack and Annie view returning to the tree house?

options.A: Both want to wait until their parents wake.
options.B: Both believe the magic person will be friendly.
options.C: Jack wants to go alone, but Annie refuses.
options.D: Annie is eager to investigate, while Jack worries about danger.

evidence: Let’s go back to the tree house right now
I don’t think we should go there
questions.rationale: Annie pushes for immediate action, while Jack imagines risks and needs persuasion.

questions.stem: What does “crept downstairs” most likely mean?

options.A: moved quietly and carefully
options.B: ran loudly and quickly
options.C: fell backward suddenly
options.D: waited beside the stairs

evidence: He crept downstairs.
Be quiet.
questions.rationale: Jack is trying not to wake his parents, so he moves quietly and carefully.

questions.stem: What can the reader infer about Jack in the dark woods?

options.A: He enjoys Annie’s jokes more than the mystery.
options.B: He no longer cares who owns the tree house.
options.C: He feels afraid but continues because Annie needs him.
options.D: He is certain the woods contain no danger.

evidence: The dark woods were a little scary.
Still holding the flashlight, she began to climb.
Annie! Come back!
questions.rationale: Jack is frightened and cautious, yet he follows Annie and keeps trying to protect her.

questions.stem: Which choice best summarizes Chapter 1?

options.A: Jack tells his parents about the medallion and waits for daylight.
options.B: Jack records the mystery, follows Annie into the woods, and reaches the tree house after her.
options.C: Annie loses her flashlight, so Jack returns home to find another one.
options.D: Jack proves who owns the tree house before he and Annie leave home.

evidence: He looked at the list he’d made before going to bed.
Jack and Annie slipped out the door
But Annie was gone.
questions.rationale: The summary includes Jack’s investigation, the trip through the woods, and Annie climbing into the tree house first.

questions.stem: What causes the tree house to begin spinning?

options.A: Jack opens the Pennsylvania book.
options.B: The knight rides beneath the tree.
options.C: Annie wishes to visit the castle.
options.D: Dawn begins to break outside.

evidence: We wish we could go there
The tree house started to spin.
questions.rationale: The magic begins immediately after Annie points to the castle picture and makes the wish.

questions.stem: Why does Jack climb down instead of returning home?

options.A: He will not leave Annie, and he also wants a quick look.
options.B: He needs to return the flashlight to the knight.
options.C: He believes the castle is safer than Frog Creek.
options.D: He wants to hide the Pennsylvania book outside.

evidence: He couldn’t leave without her.
Besides, he sort of wanted to take a peek himself.
questions.rationale: Concern for Annie and his own curiosity both motivate Jack to follow her.

questions.stem: What does “loomed out of the fog” suggest about the castle?

options.A: It disappeared behind the trees.
options.B: It moved quickly toward them.
options.C: It looked warm and welcoming.
options.D: It appeared large and shadowy.

evidence: A huge castle loomed out of the fog.
questions.rationale: The words “huge” and “fog” make the castle seem large, dark, and suddenly visible.

questions.stem: What can the reader infer about Annie after they arrive?

options.A: She regrets making the wish immediately.
options.B: She values adventure more than careful planning.
options.C: She wants Jack to explore without her.
options.D: She is more interested in Frog Creek than the castle.

evidence: Let’s stay! I want to visit the castle!
I’m going to take a peek. A teeny peek.
questions.rationale: Annie chooses immediate exploration even when Jack asks to return home and plan first.

questions.stem: Which message is best supported by Chapter 2?

options.A: Every careful plan prevents an unexpected problem.
options.B: People should avoid places they have read about.
options.C: Curiosity can pull people toward unfamiliar experiences.
options.D: Family members always make the same choices.

evidence: Then he thought about all the books in the tree house.
Besides, he sort of wanted to take a peek himself.
questions.rationale: Both children’s curiosity leads them away from home and toward the unfamiliar castle.

questions.stem: According to the book, why does a moat surround the castle?

options.A: It helps protect the castle from enemies.
options.B: It provides water for the windmill.
options.C: It gives the knight a place to train.
options.D: It separates the feast from the courtyard.

evidence: The moat was filled with water, to help protect the castle from enemies.
questions.rationale: The caption directly explains the moat’s defensive purpose.

questions.stem: How do Jack and Annie explore the castle grounds differently?

options.A: Jack follows sounds, while Annie draws a map.
options.B: Jack talks to guards, while Annie studies the book.
options.C: Jack studies information, while Annie focuses on the real sights.
options.D: Jack enters the castle, while Annie stays by the tree.

evidence: Wait. I’ll look it up
Look at the real one, Jack. Not the one in the book.
questions.rationale: Jack repeatedly uses the guidebook, while Annie wants direct experience of the real castle.

questions.stem: What does “a piercing shriek” most likely describe?

options.A: a low and gentle song
options.B: a sharp, high cry
options.C: a steady drumbeat
options.D: a quiet whisper

evidence: A piercing shriek split the air.
It sounded like it came from that little house over there!
questions.rationale: A shriek that “split the air” is a sudden, sharp, high sound.

questions.stem: What can the reader infer about Jack’s learning style?

options.A: He trusts guesses more than recorded facts.
options.B: He remembers information without checking it.
options.C: He avoids writing about unfamiliar things.
options.D: He connects book information with what he observes.

evidence: Jack studied the bridge in the picture.
Jack pulled out his notebook. He wanted to take notes
questions.rationale: Jack reads, observes the matching real features, and records questions and facts.

questions.stem: How does Annie’s final action shape the chapter?

options.A: It shifts the story from observation to entering the castle.
options.B: It resolves the mystery of the tree house owner.
options.C: It returns the children safely to Frog Creek.
options.D: It ends their interest in the castle feast.

evidence: He saw Annie dashing across the real drawbridge.
Then she vanished through the gate leading to the castle.
questions.rationale: Annie’s dash through the gate moves the plot from studying the grounds to entering a dangerous new setting.

questions.stem: Where does Jack first find Annie inside the courtyard?

options.A: beside the stable doors
options.B: under a feast table
options.C: near the giant fireplace
options.D: behind the courtyard well

evidence: She was hiding behind a well in the center of the courtyard.
questions.rationale: Jack sees Annie hiding behind the well after the horses enter the stable.

questions.stem: Why do Jack and Annie enter the bright room?

options.A: They are searching for a dry hiding place.
options.B: They want to see the feast and find the music.
options.C: They are following the knight’s black horse.
options.D: They need to ask the guard for directions.

evidence: I’m going to find the music!
The feast in the Great Hall!
questions.rationale: The sounds lead them into the castle, where they discover the feast described in the book.

questions.stem: What does Jack’s staring “in awe” show?

options.A: He is bored by the feast.
options.B: He is angry with the musicians.
options.C: He is amazed by what he sees.
options.D: He is confused about where to look.

evidence: He held his breath as he stared in awe.
A giant fireplace blazed at one end of the noisy room.
questions.rationale: The grand, unfamiliar feast amazes Jack and holds his attention.

questions.stem: What can the reader infer about the Great Hall feast?

options.A: It is lively, crowded, and unfamiliar to the children.
options.B: It is a quiet meal only for castle guards.
options.C: It has ended before Jack and Annie arrive.
options.D: It looks much like dinner at the children’s home.

evidence: Laughter and music came from a bright room
Men and women dressed in capes and furs sat at long, crowded wooden tables.
questions.rationale: Music, performers, unusual food, clothing, and crowded tables make the feast energetic and unfamiliar.

questions.stem: Which choice best summarizes Chapter 4?

options.A: Jack crosses the moat, meets the knight, and returns to the tree house.
options.B: Annie hides in the stable while Jack studies the castle book outside.
options.C: The children join the feast and quietly eat beside the castle guests.
options.D: Jack follows Annie inside, they observe the feast, and a man discovers them.

evidence: Then they slipped through the entrance of the castle.
They stood at the doorway and peeked in.
Who art thou?
questions.rationale: The central sequence is Jack finding Annie, both entering and observing the feast, and then being challenged.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of the armory?

options.A: to prepare food for the feast
options.B: to store armor and weapons
options.C: to house the knight’s horse
options.D: to hold prisoners overnight

evidence: It’s called the armory
It’s where armor and weapons are stored.
questions.rationale: Jack reads that an armory is the castle room used to store armor and weapons.

questions.stem: How does Jack’s decision to try the helmet affect events?

options.A: It helps the children look like castle guards.
options.B: It reveals a secret door behind the shelf.
options.C: It scares away the people in the hallway.
options.D: It makes him fall noisily and leads to his capture.

evidence: He tried to lift a helmet from a shelf.
Suddenly he lost his balance and went crashing into other pieces of armor.
Someone grabbed him by the arm.
questions.rationale: The heavy helmet causes Jack to crash into armor, alerting the approaching men who seize him.

questions.stem: What does “clattered to the floor” mean?

options.A: fell with a loud metallic noise
options.B: floated slowly through the room
options.C: vanished without making a sound
options.D: locked together in a neat row

evidence: Metal plates and weapons clattered to the floor.
He heard deep voices.
questions.rationale: Falling metal plates and weapons would make a loud series of hard, ringing sounds.

questions.stem: What can the reader infer about Jack in the armory?

options.A: He is focused only on finding an exit.
options.B: He expects the armor to protect Annie.
options.C: His curiosity distracts him from immediate danger.
options.D: He knows exactly how to wear a heavy helmet.

evidence: Voices came from the hallway.
I’ve got to check on something first.
He tried to lift a helmet from a shelf.
questions.rationale: Even with people approaching, Jack pauses to test the helmet, and that curiosity creates trouble.

questions.stem: Which message is best supported by Chapter 5?

options.A: Heavy objects are always more useful than light ones.
options.B: Ignoring an urgent warning can make danger worse.
options.C: A good hiding place removes every possible risk.
options.D: Learning about history prevents all mistakes.

evidence: They’re getting closer!
It’ll take just a second
Someone grabbed him by the arm.
questions.rationale: Jack ignores Annie’s urgent warning, and the short delay leads directly to being discovered and caught.

questions.stem: What makes the guards stop and kneel?

options.A: Jack promises to leave the castle.
options.B: The dungeon torch falls into water.
options.C: Annie pretends her flashlight is a magic wand.
options.D: The duke calls them back to the feast.

evidence: My magic wand!
One by one, the guards knelt down on the wet floor.
questions.rationale: The guards do not understand the flashlight and obey Annie because they believe it is magic.

questions.stem: How does Annie turn the guards’ misunderstanding into an escape?

options.A: She acts as if the flashlight has dangerous magic.
options.B: She gives the flashlight to the squinty guard.
options.C: She tells the guards that Jack is a duke.
options.D: She uses the light to find a tunnel immediately.

evidence: The guards froze. They stared at the shiny flashlight
Get down. Or I’ll wipe you out!
questions.rationale: Annie recognizes the guards’ fear and performs confidently so they believe her modern flashlight is powerful magic.

questions.stem: What does “a cold, clammy room” most likely mean?

options.A: a bright room filled with smoke
options.B: a dry room warmed by a fire
options.C: a crowded room beside the feast
options.D: a chilly room that feels damp

evidence: a cold, clammy room
Water dripped from the ceiling, making puddles
questions.rationale: The dripping water and puddles show that “clammy” means unpleasantly damp.

questions.stem: What can the reader infer about Annie in the dungeon?

options.A: She expects Jack to solve every problem.
options.B: She is quick-thinking and performs confidently under pressure.
options.C: She believes the flashlight truly has magic.
options.D: She is too frightened to speak to the guards.

evidence: Annie was quietly opening it.
Annie fiercely pointed her light
Come on, Annie whispered to Jack. Let’s go now.
questions.rationale: Annie secretly prepares the flashlight, acts convincingly, and recognizes the right moment to escape.

questions.stem: How does the flashlight episode change the chapter’s action?

options.A: It explains why the children entered the castle.
options.B: It introduces the knight before the feast begins.
options.C: It reverses the children’s situation from capture to escape.
options.D: It resolves the mystery of the letter M.

evidence: Come on, let’s chain the two of ’em
In one quick leap, Jack followed Annie out of the terrible dungeon.
questions.rationale: The children begin trapped and threatened, but Annie’s performance gives them control and an open path out.

questions.stem: How do the children locate the trapdoor?

options.A: They count five floor stones from the hallway door.
options.B: They follow the sound of water below the room.
options.C: They move every barrel in the storeroom.
options.D: They wait for a guard to open it.

evidence: The trapdoor in the picture was five stones from the door
He stamped on the fifth stone. It was loose!
questions.rationale: Jack matches the map to the room and finds the loose fifth stone.

questions.stem: How does Jack use the castle book to solve their problem?

options.A: He reads how to repair the dying flashlight.
options.B: He learns where the guards keep their horses.
options.C: He uses a map to find a hidden escape route.
options.D: He discovers a spell that opens the stone floor.

evidence: Here’s a map of the castle
There’s a trapdoor.
questions.rationale: The map identifies the storeroom, trapdoor, and secret passage that offer a way out.

questions.stem: What does “precipice” mean in this chapter?

options.A: a locked room below the castle
options.B: a steep edge above a drop
options.C: a bridge crossing the moat
options.D: a narrow shelf for armor

evidence: I can’t touch the ground.
Jack felt his fingers slipping. Then down he fell
questions.rationale: Jack hangs beyond the tunnel door with no ground beneath him, showing that a precipice is a steep drop.

questions.stem: What can the reader infer about Jack during the escape?

options.A: He refuses to use information when he feels afraid.
options.B: He depends on guards to show him the route.
options.C: He knows the passage is safe before entering.
options.D: He is resourceful but sometimes moves before knowing the risk.

evidence: Jack looked at the picture carefully.
I don’t know. We’ll find out.
I’m going to have to go feet first
questions.rationale: Jack uses the map effectively, but he enters the unknown passage and exits before understanding what a precipice is.

questions.stem: Which choice best summarizes Chapter 7?

options.A: The children hide, use a map to find a secret passage, and Jack falls from its far end.
options.B: The guards guide the children through a tunnel and safely across the moat.
options.C: Annie repairs the flashlight while Jack waits in the storeroom.
options.D: The children return to the feast and discover the knight’s identity.

evidence: Jack and Annie jumped behind the door
There’s a trapdoor.
Then down he fell through the darkness.
questions.rationale: The summary includes hiding from pursuit, discovering the passage, and the cliff-hanger fall.

questions.stem: Why does the flashlight stop working?

options.A: Jack drops it from the secret passage.
options.B: Annie leaves it in the dungeon.
options.C: The knight takes it from the backpack.
options.D: Its batteries are dead after getting wet.

evidence: Jack reached into his wet backpack and pulled out the flashlight.
The batteries were dead.
questions.rationale: The flashlight is soaked in Jack’s backpack, and its dead batteries leave them without light.

questions.stem: How does the thick mist make the children’s problem worse?

options.A: It makes the moat rise over the grass.
options.B: It hides the landmarks they need to find the tree house.
options.C: It causes the knight’s horse to run away.
options.D: It keeps their clothes from drying.

evidence: It was so misty he couldn’t see the castle.
Where was the drawbridge? The windmill? The hawk house? The grove of trees? The tree house?
questions.rationale: The mist removes every visible landmark, so they cannot tell which direction leads back to the tree house.

questions.stem: What does “swallowed up by the darkness” mean?

options.A: broken into small pieces
options.B: pulled under the moat water
options.C: completely hidden from sight
options.D: carried away by the wind

evidence: Everything had been swallowed up by the thick, soupy darkness.
questions.rationale: The darkness has not literally eaten anything; it has hidden all the landmarks from view.

questions.stem: Why does Jack believe a crocodile may be following him?

options.A: He hears splashes behind him and cannot see through the dark water.
options.B: Annie tells him she can see a crocodile near the edge.
options.C: The castle book floats past with a warning picture.
options.D: A guard shouts that crocodiles have escaped.

evidence: Another splash. Behind.
He couldn’t see anything through his water-streaked glasses.
questions.rationale: The unseen splashes and his earlier knowledge about the moat lead Jack to imagine a crocodile.

questions.stem: Which message is best supported by Chapter 8?

options.A: Fear always proves that the worst idea is true.
options.B: Working together can help people through frightening situations.
options.C: Modern tools solve every problem in an unfamiliar place.
options.D: People should wait alone when they become lost.

evidence: Take my hand!
Jack grabbed her hand. She pulled him to the edge of the moat.
questions.rationale: Jack and Annie call to each other, join hands, and reach the bank together despite fear and poor visibility.

questions.stem: How does the knight help Jack and Annie?

options.A: He carries them across the drawbridge.
options.B: He rides them back to the tree house.
options.C: He gives them a dry castle book.
options.D: He repairs Annie’s flashlight.

evidence: The knight lifted him up, too, and put him on the horse
The knight guided the horse toward the trees.
questions.rationale: The knight puts both children on his horse and delivers them to the rope ladder.

questions.stem: How do Annie and Jack first respond differently to the knight?

options.A: Annie hides, while Jack asks the knight for help.
options.B: Annie studies his armor, while Jack takes notes.
options.C: Annie wants to return to the castle, while Jack refuses.
options.D: Annie trusts his gesture, while Jack questions her.

evidence: He wants to help us
How do you know?
questions.rationale: Annie reads the knight’s gesture as help, while Jack remains unsure and asks for evidence.

questions.stem: What does “Jack froze” mean at the chapter’s beginning?

options.A: He stopped moving because he was startled.
options.B: He became covered with ice.
options.C: He ran toward the horse immediately.
options.D: He fell asleep beside the moat.

evidence: Jack froze.
Where are you going? said Jack.
questions.rationale: Jack pauses in surprise and uncertainty when the mysterious knight reaches toward them.

questions.stem: How does Jack’s feeling change during the ride?

options.A: He changes from excitement to anger at Annie.
options.B: He changes from trust to fear of the knight.
options.C: He changes from hesitation to feeling brave and powerful.
options.D: He changes from curiosity to boredom with the castle.

evidence: Jack moved slowly toward the knight.
He felt very brave and very powerful.
questions.rationale: Jack approaches cautiously, but the moonlit ride fills him with courage and wonder.

questions.stem: How does the narration’s focus on Jack affect the chapter?

options.A: Readers learn exactly what the silent knight is thinking.
options.B: Readers share Jack’s doubts and wonder during the ride.
options.C: Readers know which book Annie holds before she does.
options.D: Readers understand why the horse follows every path.

evidence: How do you know? said Jack.
He felt as if he could ride forever on this horse
questions.rationale: Jack’s questions and private feelings let readers experience his movement from uncertainty to wonder.

questions.stem: What new fact does the matching letter M reveal?

options.A: The knight built the castle and the tree house.
options.B: Annie dropped the bookmark in the castle.
options.C: One person owned the books and visited dinosaur time.
options.D: The medallion can make the tree house move.

evidence: It was the same M.
The person who had dropped the gold medallion in the time of the dinosaurs was the same person who owned all the books
questions.rationale: The identical M connects the ancient bookmark to the medallion from dinosaur time and therefore to one owner.

questions.stem: How does the bookmark change Jack’s investigation?

options.A: It connects two clues and answers part of the mystery.
options.B: It proves the knight owns Jack’s notebook.
options.C: It tells Jack how to return to the castle.
options.D: It shows that Annie imagined the whole trip.

evidence: For the first time Jack noticed a letter on the bookmark.
At least that was one mystery solved.
questions.rationale: The matching mark links two objects from different journeys and gives Jack a supported new conclusion.

questions.stem: What does “in a daze” most likely mean?

options.A: with careful excitement
options.B: with loud disappointment
options.C: with complete confidence
options.D: with a confused, stunned feeling

evidence: In a daze, Jack opened his pack.
That was close
questions.rationale: After the dangerous trip and sudden return, Jack acts while still stunned and processing what happened.

questions.stem: What can the reader infer about Jack from his final actions?

options.A: He cares more about sleep than the mystery.
options.B: He values evidence and wants to record new conclusions.
options.C: He plans to throw away the objects from the trips.
options.D: He no longer believes the tree house is magic.

evidence: Jack held the bookmark close to his lamp and studied it.
He turned to the least wet page in his notebook and started to write down this new fact.
questions.rationale: Jack examines the physical clues, draws a limited conclusion, and records the result as a fact.

questions.stem: Which choice best summarizes Chapter 10?

options.A: The children return to the castle, find the knight, and ask about the M.
options.B: The children wake their parents, explain both trips, and show them the bookmark.
options.C: The children return home, and Jack links the bookmark to the medallion before falling asleep.
options.D: Jack loses his notebook, so Annie records the tree house mystery for him.

evidence: We’re home!
It was the same M.
But before he could draw the M, his eyes closed.
questions.rationale: The chapter centers on their safe return and Jack’s discovery that the two M-marked objects share an owner.

questions.stem: What kind of noise does Jack say he heard near the tree house?

options.A: A noise that sounded like someone coughing
options.B: A noise that sounded like a bird singing
options.C: A noise that sounded like the rope ladder creaking
options.D: A noise that sounded like thunder far away

evidence: “It sounded like someone coughing.”
questions.rationale: Jack tells Annie the noise he heard sounded like someone coughing, which is why he thinks a person is nearby.

questions.stem: Why does the black cat outside the window surprise Jack and Annie so much?

options.A: It is the first cat they have ever seen in the woods
options.B: It looks exactly like the cat pictured in the Egypt book
options.C: It is chasing the mysterious M person away
options.D: It is holding the gold medallion in its mouth

evidence: "It's the cat in the Egypt book," whispered Annie.
At the end of the parade was a sleek black cat.
questions.rationale: The cat matches the black cat shown in the Egypt book's pyramid picture, so its sudden appearance stuns them.

questions.stem: In the sentence “It was very sleek and dark, with bright yellow eyes and a wide gold collar,” what does the word “sleek” most nearly mean?

options.A: Smooth and glossy
options.B: Fat and lazy
options.C: Old and sick
options.D: Loud and playful

evidence: It was very sleek and dark, with bright yellow eyes and a wide gold collar.
questions.rationale: “Sleek” describes the cat’s smooth, glossy coat, matching the elegant look paired with its bright eyes and gold collar.

questions.stem: What can the reader infer about why Jack agrees to travel into the Egypt book?

options.A: He is afraid the M person will take the book away
options.B: He especially loves pyramids and is drawn to them
options.C: Annie promises to carry his heavy backpack
options.D: He wants to escape the coughing noise in the bushes

evidence: Pyramids were high on Jack's list of favorite things, after knights and dinosaurs—plant-eating dinosaurs, that is.
He didn't have to worry about being eaten by a pyramid.
questions.rationale: The text shows pyramids are among Jack’s favorite things and feel safe to him, so he agrees to go.

questions.stem: Which sentence best summarizes what happens in this chapter?

options.A: Jack and Annie return the medallion and bookmark to the mysterious M person
options.B: Jack and Annie choose the Egypt book, and a strange black cat appears just as they wish to travel there
options.C: Jack and Annie dry out the castle book and decide to visit knights again
options.D: Jack and Annie explore the woods and never reach the tree house

evidence: She held up a book about ancient Egypt.
A black cat was sitting on a branch right outside the window.
questions.rationale: The chapter centers on the two picking the Egypt book and the mysterious black cat appearing as the tree house begins to spin.

questions.stem: Where is the tree house resting when Jack and Annie look out the window in this chapter?

options.A: On the top of a palm tree
options.B: On the top of the great stone pyramid
options.C: On a sand dune in the desert
options.D: On the back of one of the oxen

evidence: The tree house was perched on the top of a palm tree.
questions.rationale: The text states the tree house was perched on top of a palm tree among about a dozen palms.

questions.stem: How do Jack and Annie end up following the black cat to a hole in the pyramid wall?

options.A: The cat leads them straight there before the parade even appears
options.B: After the parade vanishes, they spot the cat slinking around a corner and chase it
options.C: Jack reads directions to the hole in the Egypt book
options.D: The oxen push them toward the pyramid wall

evidence: The cat started to slink away.
They dashed around the corner just in time to see the cat disappear through a hole in the pyramid wall.
questions.rationale: Once the parade disappears, the children notice the real cat slink off and chase it around the corner to the hole.

questions.stem: Based on how Jack uses it, what does the word “mirage” mean?

options.A: A real building hidden in the sand
options.B: Something that looks real but is only sunlight reflecting through heat
options.C: A ghost that haunts the desert
options.D: A kind of Egyptian funeral procession

evidence: “It must have been a mirage.”
"It looks like something's there. But it just turns out to be the sunlight reflecting through heat."
questions.rationale: Jack explains a mirage as something that seems to be there but is really sunlight reflecting through heat.

questions.stem: What can the reader infer about the black cat compared with the parade?

options.A: The cat is part of the mirage and disappears too
options.B: The cat is real, unlike the parade that vanished
options.C: The cat is a ghost that Annie fears
options.D: The cat is one of the mourners in the funeral

evidence: "He's no mirage," said Annie.
The strange parade had vanished.
questions.rationale: The parade vanishes like a mirage, but the cat remains standing and staring, so it is real.

questions.stem: Which statement best captures the main idea of this chapter?

options.A: Jack and Annie land in Egypt, watch a funeral parade vanish like a mirage, and follow the real cat into the pyramid
options.B: Jack and Annie ride with the oxen to a royal funeral inside the pyramid
options.C: Jack and Annie argue about ghosts and decide to return home to Frog Creek
options.D: Jack takes so many notes that the parade waits for them to catch up

evidence: The strange parade had vanished.
They dashed around the corner just in time to see the cat disappear through a hole in the pyramid wall.
questions.rationale: The chapter moves from arriving in Egypt, to the parade vanishing as a mirage, to chasing the real cat into the pyramid.

questions.stem: According to the Egypt book, what were pyramids mostly made of?

options.A: wooden beams and mud bricks
options.B: nearly all solid stone
options.C: sand packed into blocks
options.D: gold and painted plaster

evidence: nearly all solid stone, except for
the burial chambers deep inside.
questions.rationale: The caption Jack reads states that pyramids were nearly all solid stone.

questions.stem: How do Jack and Annie act differently as they move into the pyramid?

options.A: Jack wants to rush ahead, while Annie keeps stopping to read.
options.B: Jack keeps checking the book for directions, while Annie urges him to keep moving.
options.C: Jack refuses to enter, while Annie stays outside in the sunlight.
options.D: Jack looks for the cat, while Annie looks for a way back out.

evidence: “Wait,” said Jack. “I want to look at the book.”
“Go! Just go!” said Annie. She gave him a little push.
questions.rationale: Jack repeatedly pauses to study the book for the route, while Annie pushes him to hurry deeper inside.

questions.stem: The air deep inside felt “musty and stale.” What does this most likely mean?

options.A: fresh and cool like a breeze
options.B: old, stuffy, and not fresh
options.C: warm and full of sunlight
options.D: sweet-smelling like flowers

evidence: Soon the floor became flat. The air felt different—musty and stale.
questions.rationale: Air that is “musty and stale” deep in a sealed pyramid is old, stuffy, and not fresh.

questions.stem: What can the reader infer about how Jack and Annie know they are getting close to the burial chamber?

options.A: A guard tells them how much farther to go.
options.B: They follow the way the floor slants up and then goes flat, just like the book’s picture.
options.C: They see the mummy waiting at the doorway.
options.D: The torches on the walls suddenly go out.

evidence: The hallway slants up. Then it gets flat. Then you come to the chamber.
Soon the floor became flat.
questions.rationale: Jack matches the shape of the floor to the book’s diagram, so when it slants up then flattens, they know the chamber is near.

questions.stem: Which choice best summarizes Chapter 3?

options.A: Jack and Annie leave the pyramid and return to the tree house before dark.
options.B: Jack reads about mummies while Annie befriends a cat in the sunlight.
options.C: Jack and Annie head deep into the pyramid toward the burial chamber, and a white figure suddenly flies at them.
options.D: Jack and Annie search the desert for the entrance to a hidden pyramid.

evidence: Then he and Annie headed deeper into the pyramid.
Out of the shadows flew a white figure.
“It's alive!” Annie shouted.
questions.rationale: The chapter follows the children moving toward the burial chamber and ends with a white figure rushing at them.

questions.stem: What object does Annie pick up from the floor after the white figure passes?

options.A: a burning torch
options.B: a gold scepter with a dog's head
options.C: Jack's notebook and pencil
options.D: a piece of mummy cloth

evidence: It was a gold stick about a foot long. A dog's head was carved on one end.
"It looks like a scepter," said Jack.
questions.rationale: Annie lifts a gold stick with a dog's head carved on it, which Jack identifies as a scepter.

questions.stem: How do Annie and Jack react differently to the white figure?

options.A: Annie wants to run away, while Jack wants to chase it.
options.B: Annie believes it is a mummy and calls it back, while Jack insists it was a real person.
options.C: Annie ignores it, while Jack writes down its description.
options.D: Annie is frightened silent, while Jack shouts for help.

evidence: “Come back, mummy!” Annie called.
“That was no mummy,” said Jack. “It was a person. A real person.”
questions.rationale: Annie calls after it as a mummy she wants to help, while Jack argues it was really a person.

questions.stem: Based on the book, who is a “tomb robber”?

options.A: a king or queen who carries a scepter
options.B: a mummy that comes back from the dead
options.C: a person who steals the treasure buried with mummies
options.D: a guard who protects the pyramid

evidence: Tomb robbers often stole the treasure buried with mummies.
"Yeah, a robber who steals stuff from tombs," said Jack.
questions.rationale: The book and Jack both explain that a tomb robber is someone who steals the treasure from tombs.

questions.stem: What can the reader infer about the beautiful Egyptian lady at the end of the chapter?

options.A: She is a living queen who rules the pyramid.
options.B: She is the tomb robber in disguise.
options.C: She is not solid but a ghostly figure.
options.D: She is a statue carved from gold.

evidence: The scepter passed right through the lady's hand.
She was made of air.
questions.rationale: The scepter passes right through her hand and she is “made of air,” showing she is a ghostly, not solid, figure.

questions.stem: Which statement best expresses a theme of Chapter 4?

options.A: Careful thinking can explain a scary event, yet the world still holds real mysteries.
options.B: Stealing treasure from tombs always leads to punishment.
options.C: Kings and queens are the only people allowed inside pyramids.
options.D: It is dangerous to read books in dark places.

evidence: “Not a mummy,” he said. “Just a tomb robber.”
A wave of terror shot through him.
She was made of air.
questions.rationale: Jack reasons out that the first figure was only a robber, but then a truly unexplained ghostly lady appears, showing real mystery remains.

questions.stem: Who is the ghost that appears to Jack and Annie?

options.A: A tomb robber who stole treasure
options.B: Hutepi, a queen of the Nile
options.C: The brother who hid the book
options.D: A guard of the Underworld

evidence: "I am Hutepi," she said. "Queen of the Nile. Have you come to help me?"
questions.rationale: The ghost names herself Hutepi and says she is the Queen of the Nile.

questions.stem: Why is the ghost-queen unable to read the message her brother carved on the wall?

options.A: The message is written in a language she never learned.
options.B: Tomb robbers scratched out most of the pictures.
options.C: She cannot see things that are close to her eyes.
options.D: The room is too dark for her to see the wall.

evidence: I cannot see clearly that which is close to my eyes. I have not been able to read his message for thousands of years.
questions.rationale: The queen explains she cannot see clearly up close, which is why the message has stayed unread for so long.

questions.stem: What are "hieroglyphs," as Jack explains them in this chapter?

options.A: Magic spells spoken aloud
options.B: An ancient Egyptian way of writing with pictures
options.C: Statues built to guard a tomb
options.D: The names of Egyptian queens

evidence: "Hieroglyphs!" said Jack, finally finding his voice. "It's the ancient Egyptian way of writing. It's like writing with pictures."
questions.rationale: Jack defines hieroglyphs as the ancient Egyptian way of writing that is like writing with pictures.

questions.stem: Why can't the ghost-queen wear the glasses that Jack offers her?

options.A: The glasses are too small for her face.
options.B: She is a ghost made of air and cannot hold them.
options.C: She is afraid the glasses are cursed.
options.D: Her eyes are already too old to be helped.

evidence: She backed away from him. "I cannot wear your glasses, Jack," she said. "As you can see, I am made of air."
questions.rationale: Because she is made of air, a ghost cannot physically wear solid glasses.

questions.stem: Which sentence best summarizes what happens in Chapter 5?

options.A: Jack and Annie fight off snakes and monsters inside a tomb.
options.B: The ghost-queen teaches Jack how to speak ancient Egyptian.
options.C: The ghost-queen asks the children to help her find her Book of the Dead, and Jack begins reading the wall's picture-writing.
options.D: Annie decides the ghost is too frightening and leads Jack out of the pyramid.

evidence: "Someone must find my Book of the Dead," she said. "I need it to go on to the Next Life."
Then he stepped toward the wall and took a good long look.
questions.rationale: The chapter centers on the queen's request for help finding her Book of the Dead and Jack turning to read the carved message.

questions.stem: How many pictures are carved into the pyramid wall?

options.A: Two
options.B: Three
options.C: Four
options.D: Five

evidence: "There are four pictures here," Jack told the ghost-queen.
questions.rationale: Jack tells the ghost-queen that there are four pictures carved on the wall.

questions.stem: How do Jack and Annie help the ghost-queen understand the pictures she cannot see?

options.A: They read the ancient words out loud to her.
options.B: They describe and draw each picture so she can name it.
options.C: They carry the queen closer to the wall.
options.D: They copy the pictures onto the floor with chalk.

evidence: "Describe them to me, Jack. One at a time, please," she said.
"Wait," said Jack. "I'll draw it in my notebook. Big! So you can see it."
questions.rationale: The children describe each shape aloud, and Jack even draws one big in his notebook, so the queen can name them.

questions.stem: When Annie says the third picture is "like a thing that holds flowers," she and Jack are describing something the queen calls a jug. What is a jug?

options.A: A container for holding water
options.B: A folded piece of cloth
options.C: A set of stone stairs
options.D: A small wooden boat

evidence: "Or a thing that holds water," said Jack.
"Like a jug?" asked the ghost-queen.
questions.rationale: Jack says the shape is a thing that holds water, which the queen identifies as a jug.

questions.stem: What can the reader tell about the ghost-queen from the moment she hears the second picture might be a boat?

options.A: She is disappointed that the children guessed wrong.
options.B: She is bored and wants them to hurry.
options.C: The word boat means something important to her.
options.D: She has never heard the word boat before.

evidence: "Boat?" said the ghost-queen. She sounded excited.
The ghost-queen looked very happy. She smiled.
questions.rationale: Her excitement and happiness at the word boat suggest it matters to her and fits the message.

questions.stem: Which idea is best supported by how the children and the queen work through the writing on the wall?

options.A: Working together, people can solve a problem none could solve alone.
options.B: Ancient writing is impossible for children to understand.
options.C: It is safer to give up than to guess an answer.
options.D: Pictures are always clearer than written words.

evidence: "So that's all of them," said Annie. She pointed at each picture. "Stairs. Boat. Jug. Folded cloth."
"Come," she said. She held out her hand. "Come to my burial chambers."
questions.rationale: The queen could not see the pictures and the children could not name them alone, but together they read all four and move forward.

questions.stem: What did Jack and Annie find wrapped inside the folded cloth?

options.A: A gold plate
options.B: An ancient-looking scroll
options.C: A jeweled goblet
options.D: A small wooden statue

evidence: Jack reached into the jug and pulled out the folded cloth. It was wrapped around an ancient-looking scroll.
questions.rationale: The text states the folded cloth was wrapped around an ancient-looking scroll, so B is correct.

questions.stem: Why does Annie insist that they follow the ghost-queen and not delay leaving the scroll?

options.A: She is afraid the pyramid will collapse on them
options.B: She thinks the queen has waited thousands of years for her book
options.C: She wants to explore the false passages first
options.D: She is trying to find the way back outside

evidence: “She’s waited thousands of years for her book. Don’t make her wait anymore.”
questions.rationale: Annie's own words explain her reason: the queen has waited thousands of years, so B is correct.

questions.stem: The room is described as "cold, drafty." What does drafty most likely mean here?

options.A: Filled with bright light
options.B: Having currents of cold air moving through
options.C: Crowded with furniture
options.D: Completely sealed and airless

evidence: They stepped into a cold, drafty room.
Cold air was coming through the open doorway.
questions.rationale: The nearby clue that cold air was coming through the doorway shows drafty means air currents move through the room.

questions.stem: How can the reader tell that Jack is frightened as he crosses toward the second room?

options.A: He drops the scroll on the floor
options.B: His heart was pounding and he could barely breathe
options.C: He calls out for Queen Hutepi
options.D: He refuses to enter the pyramid at all

evidence: Jack's heart was pounding.
Jack could barely breathe.
questions.rationale: The text shows Jack's fear through his physical reactions of a pounding heart and barely being able to breathe.

questions.stem: Which sentence best summarizes the main events of this chapter?

options.A: Jack and Annie play the musical instruments they find in the pyramid
options.B: Following the ghost-queen, Jack and Annie find the Book of the Dead in a boat and carry it toward a glowing gold box
options.C: Jack and Annie get lost in a false passage and the torches go out
options.D: The ghost-queen tells Jack and Annie the history of ancient Egypt

evidence: “The Book of the Dead!” whispered Annie. “We found it.”
They walked together across the room to the glowing gold box.
questions.rationale: The chapter centers on the children following the queen, finding the scroll, and approaching the glowing gold box.

questions.stem: According to the Egypt book Jack reads, what was the first step in making a mummy?

options.A: It was covered with oil
options.B: It was wrapped tightly in bandages
options.C: It was dried out with salt
options.D: The brain was removed

evidence: First it was dried out with salt.
questions.rationale: The book states the body was first dried out with salt, so C is correct.

questions.stem: How do Jack and Annie react differently to the sight of the mummy?

options.A: Both of them are excited and want to stay
options.B: Jack finds it interesting, while Annie is disgusted and runs out
options.C: Both of them are terrified and leave together
options.D: Jack is scared, while Annie wants to examine it closely

evidence: “No,” said Jack. “It’s interesting.”
“Yuck! Stop!” cried Annie. “Good-bye!” She dashed out of the room.
questions.rationale: Jack says the mummy is interesting and stays, while Annie cries out in disgust and dashes out.

questions.stem: Jack says the passage is "one of those false passages built to fool the tomb robbers." What does false passage mean here?

options.A: A hallway that leads safely outside
options.B: A hidden room full of treasure
options.C: A fake hallway made to trick and mislead people
options.D: A staircase that only the queen can use

evidence: “I think this is one of those false passages built to fool the tomb robbers,” said Jack.
“Yeah, it looks just like the other hallway,” said Jack.
questions.rationale: Jack explains the passage was built to fool tomb robbers and only looks like the real hallway.

questions.stem: Why does Jack first run in the wrong direction when he hears Annie calling for help?

options.A: He wants to leave the pyramid without her
options.B: Her voice grew fainter, showing he was moving away from her
options.C: He is following the ghost-queen instead
options.D: He goes back to place the scroll by the mummy

evidence: “Help, Jack!” Her cry seemed fainter.
He was running away from her voice.
questions.rationale: The clue that her cry seemed fainter tells Jack he was running away from her, so he turns back.

questions.stem: What lesson about the two children does the ending of this chapter best show?

options.A: Curiosity about mummies is always dangerous
options.B: Jack cares about Annie and goes back to rescue her even when he is scared
options.C: Tomb robbers are the real danger inside the pyramid
options.D: It is better to explore a pyramid alone than together

evidence: He went back toward the burial chambers.
“Jack!” Annie was running through the hallway toward him. She crashed into him.
questions.rationale: When Jack realizes Annie is lost, he turns back toward the burial chambers to find her, showing his care.

questions.stem: What finally leads Jack and Annie out of the dark passage?

options.A: They push the wooden door until it opens.
options.B: They follow the cat's meow through the hallway.
options.C: They light a torch to see the way out.
options.D: They dig a tunnel through the stone wall.

evidence: “Follow him!” cried Jack. “He’s going away from us.”
Jack and Annie kept following the sound, all the way through the winding hallway.
questions.rationale: Jack and Annie escape by following the cat's meow through the winding hallway to the exit.

questions.stem: How does hearing the cat's meow change what Jack and Annie do?

options.A: It makes them stop pushing and give up hope.
options.B: It makes them run back to the wooden door.
options.C: It makes them stand still and wait to be rescued.
options.D: It makes them move again and follow the sound.

evidence: They were trapped.
Jack and Annie started down the dark hallway, following the cat's meow.
questions.rationale: Just when they feel trapped, the meow gives them a direction, so they start moving again to follow it.

questions.stem: In “It wouldn't budge,” what does “budge” mean?

options.A: make a loud noise
options.B: move even a little
options.C: break into pieces
options.D: lock up tightly

evidence: They leaned against the wooden door and pushed.
It wouldn't budge.
questions.rationale: They push the door but it will not move at all, so “budge” means to move even slightly.

questions.stem: What can readers infer about how Jack feels while trapped in the dark?

options.A: He is calm and completely unafraid.
options.B: He is frightened but tries to control it.
options.C: He is excited to explore the dark tunnel.
options.D: He is angry at Annie for the trouble.

evidence: "Don't worry. Everything's going to be okay," said Jack. He was trying to stay calm.
His heart was pounding as he tried to see through the darkness.
questions.rationale: His pounding heart shows fear, while his effort to “stay calm” shows he is trying to control that fear.

questions.stem: Which choice best summarizes the chapter?

options.A: Jack and Annie open a stuck door on their own, then walk calmly home in the dark.
options.B: Trapped in darkness, Jack and Annie follow the cat to safety, then ride the tree house home.
options.C: The cat leads Jack and Annie deeper into the pyramid, where they decide to stay the night.
options.D: Annie finds an exit while Jack stays behind to watch a boat glide across the sand.

evidence: They were trapped.
Jack and Annie kept following the sound, all the way through the winding hallway.
"I wish we could go home," he said.
questions.rationale: The chapter's main sequence is being trapped, following the cat out, and then wishing the tree house home.

questions.stem: What does Jack see shining on the wooden floor of the tree house?

options.A: The letter M
options.B: A gold medallion
options.C: A leather bookmark
options.D: A tiny cat

evidence: It was the letter M! It shimmered in the sunlight.
questions.rationale: When Jack catches the light just right, he sees the shining letter M on the floor.

questions.stem: Why does finding the letter M matter to Jack and Annie?

options.A: It shows the tree house is about to disappear.
options.B: It proves the tree house belongs to M.
options.C: It tells them which book to read next.
options.D: It warns them to leave the woods quickly.

evidence: This absolutely proved the tree house belonged to M. There was no doubt about it.
The person who seems to own all the books in the tree house?
questions.rationale: The M is a clue that settles who owns the tree house, confirming it belongs to the mysterious M.

questions.stem: In “It came into focus,” what does “focus” mean here?

options.A: became clear to see
options.B: started to move
options.C: grew very bright
options.D: disappeared slowly

evidence: He tipped his head a bit more. It came into focus.
It was the letter M!
questions.rationale: After Jack tips his head, the shape becomes clear enough to recognize as the letter M.

questions.stem: What can readers infer about why Jack speaks loudly to the bushes?

options.A: He is lost and calling for his mother.
options.B: He believes M is close and can hear him.
options.C: He is frightened of an animal in the woods.
options.D: He wants Annie to stop and listen to him.
