evidence: "I feel like M is nearby," Jack whispered.
"I'm going to bring the medallion back soon," Jack said loudly. "And the bookmark, too. Both of them. Tomorrow!"
questions.rationale: Jack senses M is near, so he speaks loudly on purpose so that M might hear his promise.

questions.stem: Which statement best expresses a theme of this final chapter?

options.A: Solving one mystery can leave an even bigger mystery to explore.
options.B: Friends should always keep their adventures a secret.
options.C: It is safer to stay home than to explore new places.
options.D: Hard work is always rewarded with a good lunch.

evidence: This absolutely proved the tree house belonged to M. There was no doubt about it.
Will we ever meet M? Jack wondered.
questions.rationale: The clue answers who owns the tree house, yet the unanswered question of who M is keeps the larger mystery open.

questions.stem: What two objects does Jack take out of his drawer to bring to the tree house?

options.A: His raincoat and rain boots
options.B: The gold medallion and the leather bookmark
options.C: His notebook and a flashlight
options.D: A castle book and a dinosaur book

evidence: Jack reached into his drawer. He took out the medallion.
Then he took out the bookmark. It was made of blue leather.
questions.rationale: Jack goes to his drawer and takes out the gold medallion and then the blue leather bookmark to carry to the tree house.

questions.stem: How does the green parrot named Polly cause the children's adventure to begin?

options.A: It flies into the tree house and knocks the open book onto the floor
options.B: It appears right after Annie wishes to be in the picture, and then the tree house starts to spin
options.C: It carries the medallion away, so they chase it into the woods
options.D: It reads the title of the book out loud to Jack and Annie

evidence: Annie pointed to the picture. "I wish we were there instead of here," she said.
The tree house started to spin. Faster and faster!
questions.rationale: The parrot from the picture shows up just after Annie makes her wish, and moments later the wind rises and the tree house spins, carrying them away.

questions.stem: In the sentence "The letter M was engraved on it," what does the word "engraved" most nearly mean?

options.A: Cut or carved into the surface
options.B: Painted with bright colors
options.C: Wrapped in leather
options.D: Glued on as a sticker

evidence: It was gold. The letter M was engraved on it.
questions.rationale: The M is engraved on the gold medallion, meaning it is cut or carved into the metal, which is why it matches the M on the tree-house floor.

questions.stem: What can the reader infer about the place shown in the open book Annie finds?

options.A: It is a cold, snowy mountain far from the sea
options.B: It is a warm, sunny place by the ocean with a beach, a palm tree, and a ship
options.C: It is the same rainy Frog Creek woods they just left
options.D: It is a busy city full of tall buildings

evidence: He saw a sunny beach. A big green parrot sitting in a palm tree. And a ship sailing on a blue sea.
Annie pointed to the picture. "I wish we were there instead of here," she said.
questions.rationale: The picture shows a sunny beach, a palm tree, and a ship on a blue sea, so the reader can tell it is a warm place by the ocean—very different from the cold rain outside.

questions.stem: Which sentence best summarizes what happens in this chapter?

options.A: Jack and Annie go to the tree house in the rain, find an open book with a sunny sea, and the tree house spins them away
options.B: Jack and Annie decide it is too wet and stay home instead of visiting the tree house
options.C: Jack and Annie return the castle book and dinosaur book to the M person
options.D: Jack and Annie teach the green parrot Polly how to talk

evidence: But we have to go to the tree house," said Annie.
The tree house started to spin. Faster and faster!
questions.rationale: The chapter follows the two hurrying through the storm to the tree house, discovering the open book that shows a sunny sea, and being whisked away as the tree house spins.

questions.stem: What does Jack notice about the sailing ship's flag when the ship comes closer?

options.A: It is bright green with a palm tree on it
options.B: It is black with a skull and crossbones
options.C: It is white with a large letter M
options.D: It is red and yellow like a rainbow

evidence: The flag was black. With a skull and crossbones.
questions.rationale: As the ship nears, Jack can see its flag, which is black with a skull and crossbones—the sign of pirates.

questions.stem: How do Jack and Annie act differently once the tree house lands by the sea?

options.A: Both of them refuse to leave the tree house at all
options.B: Annie wants to run into the water right away, while Jack wants to study the book first
options.C: Jack jumps in first, and Annie stays behind to read
options.D: Both of them decide to swim out to the sailing ship

evidence: "Come on, let's follow her! Let's go in the water!" said Annie.
“Wait, we have to study the book first,” said Jack.
questions.rationale: Annie is eager to jump straight into the water, but careful Jack wants to study the book before doing anything, showing how their reactions differ.

questions.stem: In the sentence "He shielded his eyes against the sun," what does "shielded" most nearly mean?

options.A: Covered or protected
options.B: Rubbed and scratched
options.C: Closed and rested
options.D: Washed with water

evidence: He shielded his eyes against the sun. And peered out at the sea.
questions.rationale: Jack shields his eyes against the bright sun, meaning he covers or protects them with his hand so he can peer out at the sea.

questions.stem: Why does a chill go through Jack when he sees the ship's flag?

options.A: He realizes the ship is sailing away from them
options.B: He realizes the ship belongs to pirates and may be dangerous
options.C: He is cold because his clothes are still wet from the rain
options.D: He is worried the ship will crash into the palm trees

evidence: The flag was black. With a skull and crossbones.
"Pirates of the Caribbean," Jack read aloud.
questions.rationale: The skull-and-crossbones flag and the book's title tell Jack the ship is a pirate ship, so the chill shows he senses danger coming toward them.

questions.stem: What lesson does this chapter most suggest through what happens to Jack and Annie?

options.A: A beautiful, fun place can still hide a real danger you do not notice at first
options.B: It is always better to swim than to read a book
options.C: You should never leave home when it is raining
options.D: Parrots make the best guides on an island

evidence: Actually, the water did look wonderful.
As he stared at the ship's flag, a chill went through him.
questions.rationale: The children enjoy the wonderful warm water, but the approaching pirate flag shows that even a lovely place can hide danger they only spot too late.

questions.stem: According to the caption Jack reads, what did pirates do three hundred years ago?

options.A: They built stone forts along the coast.
options.B: They raided Spanish treasure ships in the Caribbean Sea.
options.C: They mapped every island in the sea.
options.D: They traded gold with the Spanish navy.

evidence: Three hundred years ago, pirates raided Spanish treasure ships in the Caribbean Sea.
questions.rationale: The caption states directly that pirates raided Spanish treasure ships in the Caribbean Sea.

questions.stem: How do Jack and Annie react differently when Annie first sees the pirates?

options.A: Both of them run for the tree house right away.
options.B: Jack hides in the sand while Annie waves at the pirates.
options.C: Annie draws the flag while Jack watches the boat.
options.D: Annie warns Jack and starts running, but Jack keeps drawing instead of looking up.

evidence: "Jack, some pirates are getting into a rowboat," said Annie.
Jack kept drawing.
questions.rationale: Annie notices the danger and calls out, but Jack is so focused on his drawing that he keeps at it instead of reacting.

questions.stem: Jack saw something "glinting in the sand." What does glinting most likely mean here?

options.A: shining with small flashes of light
options.B: sinking down slowly
options.C: making a loud noise
options.D: moving on its own

evidence: Jack saw something glinting in the sand. He reached for it. It was his glasses.
questions.rationale: Because the object turns out to be his glasses catching the light, "glinting" means shining with small flashes of light.

questions.stem: Why does Jack run back toward the water even though the pirates are coming?

options.A: He wants to fight the pirates by himself.
options.B: He is trying to hide the rowboat from Annie.
options.C: He left the pirate book propped in the sand and does not want to lose it.
options.D: He dropped his glasses near the shore.

evidence: Suddenly Jack saw the pirate book. In all the confusion he had forgotten it. It was still propped in the sand.
"I'll be right back!" Jack called. "I've got to get the book!"
questions.rationale: Jack realizes he forgot the pirate book in the sand, so he runs back to grab it before it is lost.

questions.stem: Which choice best summarizes Chapter 3?

options.A: Jack and Annie sail out to meet the pirate ship and make friends with the crew.
options.B: Jack learns about pirates and draws their flag, then he and Annie must flee as three pirates row ashore and charge at them.
options.C: Annie reads about the Jolly Roger while Jack looks for his lost glasses in the sand.
options.D: Jack and Annie find pirate treasure buried under a palm tree on the beach.

evidence: He started drawing the Jolly Roger flag.
Three big pirates splashed onto the sand.
questions.rationale: The chapter moves from Jack studying pirates and drawing their flag to the children fleeing as three pirates land and rush at them.

questions.stem: What does the biggest pirate call himself?

options.A: Pinky
options.B: Stinky
options.C: Cap'n Bones
options.D: Jolly Roger

evidence: "No one escapes Cap'n Bones!" he roared. His breath was terrible.
questions.rationale: The biggest pirate roars that no one escapes Cap'n Bones, naming himself.

questions.stem: What does Annie do that shows she is brave even after Cap'n Bones grabs Jack?

options.A: She charges up to the biggest pirate, calls him a bully, and hits and kicks him.
options.B: She quietly climbs back up into the tree house.
options.C: She offers the pirates the medallion so they will let Jack go.
options.D: She runs down the beach to get help from Pinky and Stinky.

evidence: "Leave him alone, you bully!" she cried.
She hit the pirate with her fist and kicked him.
questions.rationale: Instead of hiding, Annie confronts the huge pirate, insults him, and physically fights back, showing her courage.

questions.stem: The chapter is titled "Vile Booty," and Cap'n Bones keeps yelling about wanting gold, not books. In pirate talk, what does booty mean?

options.A: a pirate's boots
options.B: an old sailing map
options.C: a kind of sea bird
options.D: stolen treasure or valuable loot

evidence: "Aghh, books," growled Cap'n Bones. He spit on the sand. "I want gold, you dogs!"
"Gold! Gold! Gold!" he cried.
questions.rationale: Cap'n Bones treasures gold and scorns books, so "booty" means the stolen treasure or valuable loot pirates hunt for.

questions.stem: What can the reader infer about why Cap'n Bones suddenly drops Jack and Annie?

options.A: He decides the children are not worth his time.
options.B: He sees the shiny gold medallion and cares far more about the treasure than about them.
options.C: He hears his ship calling him back to sea.
options.D: He is frightened by the gleeful shriek from the tree house.

evidence: Pinky leaned out the tree house window. He held the medallion. It glimmered in the sunlight.
Cap'n Bones dropped Jack and Annie. He caught the medallion as it fell.
questions.rationale: The moment the gold medallion appears, Cap'n Bones lets the children go to grab it, showing greed for treasure outweighs holding them.

questions.stem: Which statement best expresses a theme suggested by Chapter 4?

options.A: Greed for gold can control a person, making treasure matter more to them than anything else.
options.B: Reading books is a waste of a pirate's time.
options.C: It is always safer to give bullies whatever they ask for.
options.D: The biggest and strongest person always wins in the end.

evidence: "I hate books! Keep looking, dogs! Find me something good!"
"Gold! Gold! Gold!" he cried. Cap'n Bones threw back his head and laughed horribly.
questions.rationale: Cap'n Bones ignores everything but gold and forgets the children the instant treasure appears, showing how greed can take over a person.

questions.stem: What does Cap'n Bones pull out of his belt pouch to show Jack and Annie?

options.A: A gold coin from the treasure
options.B: A torn treasure map
options.C: A loaded pistol
options.D: A letter from Captain Kidd

evidence: He reached into a belt pouch and pulled out a torn piece of paper. He waved it at Jack and Annie.
"Is that a treasure map?" asked Jack.
questions.rationale: Bones pulls a torn piece of paper from his pouch, and Jack recognizes it as a treasure map.

questions.stem: Why does Cap'n Bones make Jack read the map instead of reading it himself?

options.A: Bones cannot read, so he needs the children to read it for him.
options.B: Bones wants to test whether the children are telling the truth.
options.C: The writing on the map is in a language only Jack knows.
options.D: Bones is too busy aiming his pistols to hold the map.

evidence: "You know he can't read," said Stinky.
“You read it!” Cap'n Bones shoved the map in Jack's face.
questions.rationale: Stinky reveals that Bones can't read, which is why Bones shoves the map at Jack to read aloud.

questions.stem: When Cap'n Bones shouts "Halt!" and "Not another step, lubbers!", what is he telling Jack and Annie to do?

options.A: To come closer to him
options.B: To stop moving and stay where they are
options.C: To climb into the tree house
options.D: To hand over the map

evidence: “Halt!” Cap’n Bones shouted. He aimed his pistols at them. “Not another step, lubbers!”
Jack and Annie froze.
questions.rationale: "Halt" and "not another step" mean to stop; Jack and Annie freeze in response.

questions.stem: Why does Cap'n Bones decide to take Jack and Annie back to the ship?

options.A: He wants to reward them for reading the map.
options.B: He believes they know how to find Kidd's treasure and will hold them until they tell him.
options.C: He is afraid the coming storm will hurt them on the island.
options.D: He plans to set them free on the far side of the island.

evidence: “Hang it! Take 'em hack to the ship!” shouted Cap'n Bones. "They can rot there till they're ready
questions.rationale: Bones has them taken to the ship to hold them until they reveal how to find Kidd's treasure, showing he thinks they know.

questions.stem: Which sentence best summarizes what happens in Chapter 5?

options.A: Jack and Annie escape the pirates and reach the tree house safely.
options.B: Cap'n Bones traps Jack and Annie, makes Jack read the map's clue, and has them rowed out to his ship.
options.C: Polly the parrot leads Jack and Annie to Captain Kidd's buried treasure.
options.D: Cap'n Bones reads the map himself and digs up the gold on the island.

evidence: The pirate squinted his good eye. "Aye, lubber. When the treasure's in me hands, I'll let you go."
Jack and Annie were tossed into the rowboat.
questions.rationale: The chapter shows the pirates capturing the children, forcing Jack to read the clue, and taking them by rowboat toward the ship.

questions.stem: Where do the pirates lock Jack and Annie after hauling them onto the ship?

options.A: In the ship's cabin
options.B: Below deck with the treasure
options.C: In the rowboat
options.D: In a cage on the deck

evidence: "Lock 'em in my cabin!" Cap'n Bones ordered.
A couple of pirates grabbed Jack and Annie. And threw them in the ship's cabin. Then locked the door.
questions.rationale: Bones orders them locked in his cabin, and the pirates throw them in and lock the door.

questions.stem: How do Jack and Annie finally understand who the "kid" in Cap'n Bones's clue really is?

options.A: Polly the parrot spells out the name for them.
options.B: Cap'n Bones tells them the treasure belonged to his son.
options.C: They read in the pirate book about Captain Kidd, a pirate who buried treasure.
options.D: Annie remembers the name from a picture on the map.

evidence: Captain Kidd was a famous pirate. It is said that he buried a treasure chest on a deserted island. The chest was filled with gold and jewels.
"So that's the kid that Bones keeps talking about," said Annie.
questions.rationale: Reading about the pirate Captain Kidd in the book lets Annie realize "Kidd" is the "kid" Bones keeps mentioning.

questions.stem: In this chapter, what does it mean that Jack "felt seasick"?

options.A: He felt sick because of the tossing, rocking boat on the huge waves.
options.B: He was tired of looking at the sea.
options.C: He was frightened by the pirates' music.
options.D: He had eaten spoiled food on the ship.

evidence: The rowboat tossed from side to side. The waves were huge. Salty spray stung Jack's eyes. He felt seasick.
questions.rationale: The tossing boat and huge waves make Jack "seasick" - sick from the motion of the sea.

questions.stem: Why is Jack so amazed when he finally looks out the window at the island?

options.A: He spots Polly the parrot leading the way home.
options.B: The island is shaped like a giant whale, so its "eye" matches the clue on the map.
options.C: He realizes the ship has drifted far from the island.
options.D: He sees the pirates already digging up the treasure.

evidence: “There! The island is shaped like a giant whale!”
“The gold doth lie beneath the whale’s eye,” whispered Jack. “Wow.”
questions.rationale: Jack is amazed because the whale-shaped island's "eye" (a big black rock) matches the map's clue about where the gold lies.

questions.stem: What big idea does this chapter suggest about how Jack and Annie face their trouble?

options.A: Fighting the pirates with swords is the only way to win.
options.B: By staying calm and using the book, thinking, and noticing clues, they begin to solve the mystery even while trapped.
options.C: Waiting quietly for the storm to pass will set them free.
options.D: The treasure matters more to them than getting home.

evidence: “We better examine the book,” he said.
“There! The island is shaped like a giant whale!”
questions.rationale: Locked in the cabin, the children turn to the book and careful observation to decode the clue, showing that thinking and noticing details help them face danger.

questions.stem: What two things does Cap'n Bones order his men to bring before they head back to the island?

options.A: A map and a lantern
options.B: A rope and shovels
options.C: A sword and a spyglass
options.D: Food and fresh water

evidence: "You'll need a rope," said Jack.
"And shovels," said Annie.
questions.rationale: Jack and Annie tell Cap'n Bones they will need a rope and shovels, and he roars at his men to get them.

questions.stem: Why does Cap'n Bones keep holding on to Jack and Annie instead of helping dig?

options.A: He wants to protect them from the coming storm
options.B: He plans to keep them as hostages until he has the treasure
options.C: He is too tired from rowing to lift a shovel
options.D: He is afraid they will steal his gold medallion

evidence: Cap'n Bones gave Jack a horrible grin. "Nay. I'm going to hold you two--till there's treasure in me hands!"
questions.rationale: He refuses to work and grins that he will hold the two children until the treasure is in his hands, using them to make sure he gets it.

questions.stem: When Pinky says "Gale's a-blowin'!" as the waves grow bigger and the wind howls, what is a gale?

options.A: A strong, powerful wind or storm
options.B: A calm, gentle breeze
options.C: A patch of thick fog
options.D: A large ocean wave

evidence: The waves were bigger. The wind was howling.
"Gale's a-blowin'!" said Pinky.
questions.rationale: Pinky calls out about the gale just as the wind howls and the waves grow, showing a gale is a strong wind or storm.

questions.stem: What can the reader infer about Jack's feelings when Cap'n Bones says he will hold them until he has the treasure?

options.A: Jack feels relieved that the pirates will do all the digging
options.B: Jack feels frightened and is trying to think of a way to escape
options.C: Jack feels excited to watch the pirates work
options.D: Jack feels proud that he tricked Cap'n Bones

evidence: Jack gulped. How could they get away from him?
questions.rationale: Jack gulps and wonders how they can get away, which shows he is scared and looking for a way to escape.

questions.stem: Which sentence best summarizes what happens in this chapter?

options.A: Jack and Annie lead Cap'n Bones back to the island through a rising storm, but he holds them prisoner instead of letting them slip away to the tree house
options.B: Jack and Annie dig up Captain Kidd's treasure chest by themselves while the pirates sail away
options.C: Cap'n Bones frees Jack and Annie once they point out the black rock
options.D: Pinky and Stinky refuse to row, so the pirates never reach the island

evidence: "Now show us where the treasure is."
"Nay. I'm going to hold you two--till there's treasure in me hands!"
questions.rationale: The chapter follows the children guiding the pirates back through the gale to the black rock, where Cap'n Bones keeps them captive rather than freeing them.

questions.stem: What do Pinky and Stinky do first before they begin digging?

options.A: They tie their rope around the big rock and pull it across the sand
options.B: They row the boat back out to the ship
options.C: They climb up the tree house ladder
options.D: They toss the gold medallion into the hole

evidence: Pinky and Stinky tied their rope around the big rock.
They started pulling the rock across the sand.
questions.rationale: Before digging, the two pirates tie their rope around the big rock and drag it across the sand.

questions.stem: How does Polly the parrot cause Pinky and Stinky to stop digging and run away?

options.A: She drops the medallion into the sea in front of them
options.B: She keeps squawking "Go back!", which the pirates take as a warning to reach the ship
options.C: She attacks Cap'n Bones so the men can escape
options.D: She shows them a shortcut to the buried treasure

evidence: "Go back!" she squawked.
"The bird's warning us!" shouted Pinky. "We've got to get to the ship before it's too late!"
questions.rationale: Polly's repeated "Go back!" convinces the superstitious pirates the bird is warning them, so they throw down their shovels and run for the boat.

questions.stem: When Stinky shouts "The bird's an omen, Cap'n!" during the coming storm, what does the word "omen" most nearly mean?

options.A: A sign that something is going to happen
options.B: A kind of tropical bird
options.C: A tool used for digging
options.D: A pirate captain's order

evidence: "The bird's an omen, Cap'n!" shouted Stinky.
"The bird's warning us!" shouted Pinky.
questions.rationale: The pirates treat the parrot as an omen, a sign that danger is coming, which is why Pinky says the bird is warning them.

questions.stem: What can the reader infer about why Jack finally decides it is time to leave the treasure chest behind?

options.A: He realizes the chest is empty and worthless
options.B: The parrot speaks to him like a person he seems to know, and he trusts her warning
options.C: Cap'n Bones comes back to grab him again
options.D: Annie takes the gold medallion away from him

evidence: "Go back, Jack," she said. She sounded like a person.
Okay. It was definitely time to go.
questions.rationale: When Polly says his name and sounds like a person he seems to know, Jack decides it is definitely time to go, showing he trusts her.

questions.stem: Which statement best expresses a theme suggested by this chapter?

options.A: Riches are always worth any danger you must face to get them
options.B: Sometimes you must let go of what you want and heed a warning to stay safe
options.C: Pirates are the bravest people on the sea
options.D: Storms only strike those who have done something wrong

evidence: "Forget the treasure chest!" said Annie. "We have to go now! The storm's getting worse!"
"Go back, Jack," she said.
questions.rationale: Though Jack finds the treasure chest, the worsening storm and Polly's warning push him to leave it behind and get to safety, suggesting that heeding a warning matters more than greed.

questions.stem: When Jack opens his eyes at the start of the chapter, where are he and Annie?

options.A: Still on the pirates' ship
options.B: Back in Frog Creek
options.C: On the whale-shaped island
options.D: Inside King Arthur's castle

evidence: They were back in Frog Creek. The rain was softer. The wind was gentler.
questions.rationale: The text states plainly that they were back in Frog Creek, where the rain and wind were now gentle.

questions.stem: Why does Annie feel sad right after they return home?

options.A: She lost the gold medallion in the sea
options.B: She wishes Polly the parrot had come back with them
options.C: She is angry that Jack kept the pirate book
options.D: She did not get to dig up the treasure chest

evidence: "Polly's gone," said Annie sadly. "I was hoping she might come back with us."
questions.rationale: Annie is sad because Polly is gone and she had hoped the parrot would return home with them.

questions.stem: When Polly "swooshed into the tree house" and "perched on the stack of books," what does perched mean?

options.A: Settled and rested on top of something
options.B: Pecked angrily at something
options.C: Flew away in fear
options.D: Fell down onto the floor

evidence: The parrot swooshed into the tree house. She perched on the stack of books.
questions.rationale: To perch means to settle and rest on top of something, which is what the parrot does on the stack of books.

questions.stem: What surprising thing happens to Polly at the end of the chapter?

options.A: She flies back to the Caribbean island
options.B: She turns into the gold medallion
options.C: She changes from a parrot into an old woman named Morgan le Fay
options.D: She leads Jack and Annie to the treasure chest

evidence: Polly was not a parrot any longer. In her place was an old woman.
"My name is Morgan le Fay."
questions.rationale: In a swirl of feathers and light, Polly transforms into an old woman who says her name is Morgan le Fay.

questions.stem: Which sentence best summarizes Chapter 9?

options.A: Jack and Annie arrive home to sunny Frog Creek and decide to keep the pirate treasure
options.B: Back home in the rain, Jack and Annie put away the pirate book and wonder about the mysterious M, when Polly returns and changes into Morgan le Fay
options.C: Jack and Annie sail back to the island to search for the letter M
options.D: Polly the parrot flies off forever and never returns to the tree house

evidence: "We didn't find any M's on this trip," he said.
Then, in a great swirl of colors—in a blur of feathers and light... a new being took shape.
questions.rationale: The chapter shows the children back home wondering about the M person, then witnessing Polly transform into Morgan le Fay.

questions.stem: Who does Morgan le Fay say she is?

options.A: A pirate queen from the Caribbean
options.B: King Arthur's sister from Camelot
options.C: Jack and Annie's grandmother
options.D: The captain of the treasure ship

evidence: "Well, I am King Arthur's sister," said Morgan.
"You're from Camelot," said Jack.
questions.rationale: Morgan tells them she is King Arthur's sister, and Jack recognizes that she is from Camelot.

questions.stem: According to Morgan, what two reasons let Jack and Annie "hook into" her magic and use the tree house?

options.A: Annie is brave and Jack is strong
options.B: Annie believes in magic and Jack loves books
options.C: They both found the letter M and the medallion
options.D: They were both born in Camelot long ago

evidence: "First, Annie believes in magic. So she actually saw the tree house."
"you picked up a book, Jack. And because you love books so much, you caused my magic spell to work."
questions.rationale: Morgan explains that Annie's belief in magic let her see the tree house, and Jack's love of books made the spell work.

questions.stem: Morgan says, "Most call me an enchantress. But I'm also a librarian." What is an enchantress?

options.A: A woman who uses magic or casts spells
options.B: A woman who sails pirate ships
options.C: A woman who paints pictures in books
options.D: A woman who takes care of a garden

evidence: "Most call me an enchantress."
"I placed a spell on the tree house," said Morgan.
questions.rationale: Morgan casts a spell on the tree house, showing that an enchantress is a woman who uses magic or casts spells.

questions.stem: After the tree house vanishes, Jack finds the gold medallion back in his pocket. What does Annie think this means?

options.A: That the medallion was fake all along
options.B: That a pirate slipped it there
options.C: That Morgan put it there and will be coming back
options.D: That they never really left Frog Creek

evidence: "Morgan must have put it there," she said.
"Magic," said Annie. "I think it means she'll be coming back."
questions.rationale: Annie says Morgan must have placed the medallion in his pocket by magic, and takes it as a sign Morgan will return.

questions.stem: The book ends with Jack thinking, "Forget the treasure chest. They had treasure at home." What theme does this ending suggest?

options.A: Pirate gold is the greatest treasure of all
options.B: The real treasure is the beauty and wonder of home and everyday life
options.C: You should always keep searching for buried treasure
options.D: Magic is more important than family

evidence: Forget the treasure chest.

Leaves, branches, puddles, bushes, grass, vines, wild flowers—all glittered like jewels.
Or gleamed like gold.
questions.rationale: As the ordinary woods sparkle like jewels and gold around him, Jack realizes the true treasure is the wonder of home, not the pirate chest.
